SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims (L1-norm based whole-filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies, and practical handling for ResNets) are backed by empirical results on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012 and by comparisons to sensible baselines (random, largest-filter, activation-based), including FLOP and wall-clock measurements. There are no formal mathematical derivations required for the heuristic L1 criterion, and the methodology appears sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and iterative retraining costs are acknowledged as a practical limitation. These are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental conclusions. | B(4): The paper clearly states and experimentally supports its main claim that L1-norm based whole-filter pruning reduces FLOPs with little accuracy loss (results reported on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012, and comparisons to random and activation-based baselines are included). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the proposed handling for residual blocks and the stage-wise/one-shot strategies are described and empirically validated. Minor concerns remain about experimental rigor (no mention of statistical significance, run-to-run variance, or full reporting details for very deep networks), but these do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims (L1-norm based whole-filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies, and practical handling for ResNets) are backed by empirical results on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012 and by comparisons to sensible baselines (random, largest-filter, activation-based), including FLOP and wall-clock measurements. There are no formal mathematical derivations required for the heuristic L1 criterion, and the methodology appears sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and iterative retraining costs are acknowledged as a practical limitation. These are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and experimentally supports its main claim that L1-norm based whole-filter pruning reduces FLOPs with little accuracy loss (results reported on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012, and comparisons to random and activation-based baselines are included). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the proposed handling for residual blocks and the stage-wise/one-shot strategies are described and empirically validated. Minor concerns remain about experimental rigor (no mention of statistical significance, run-to-run variance, or full reporting details for very deep networks), but these do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clear, practically-oriented new method—pruning entire convolutional filters based on their L1-norm—to obtain structured sparsity (avoiding unstructured sparse libraries) and presents one-shot and stage-wise strategies plus practical handling for residual blocks. It differentiates itself from prior activation-based feature-map pruning and group-sparse regularization, and demonstrates strong empirical gains (FLOP and wall-clock reductions) on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. The idea is relatively simple rather than highly theoretical, but it is a notable and useful departure from existing unstructured/activation-based approaches that would inform practitioners and researchers. | B(4): The paper proposes a concrete, different approach—pruning entire convolutional filters by their L1-norm—to avoid unstructured sparsity and the need for sparse libraries, alongside practical one-shot and stage-wise pruning strategies and special handling for residual blocks. It clearly contrasts this filter-level, weight-norm criterion with prior random, activation-based, or group-sparse methods and reports practical FLOP and wall-clock gains on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012. These elements together represent a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a purely incremental combination of known techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clear, practically-oriented new method—pruning entire convolutional filters based on their L1-norm—to obtain structured sparsity (avoiding unstructured sparse libraries) and presents one-shot and stage-wise strategies plus practical handling for residual blocks. It differentiates itself from prior activation-based feature-map pruning and group-sparse regularization, and demonstrates strong empirical gains (FLOP and wall-clock reductions) on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. The idea is relatively simple rather than highly theoretical, but it is a notable and useful departure from existing unstructured/activation-based approaches that would inform practitioners and researchers.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a concrete, different approach—pruning entire convolutional filters by their L1-norm—to avoid unstructured sparsity and the need for sparse libraries, alongside practical one-shot and stage-wise pruning strategies and special handling for residual blocks. It clearly contrasts this filter-level, weight-norm criterion with prior random, activation-based, or group-sparse methods and reports practical FLOP and wall-clock gains on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012. These elements together represent a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a purely incremental combination of known techniques.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the summary, the paper is well organized with a clear flow from method (L1-norm filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies, ResNet handling) to experiments (FLOPs, accuracy, wall-clock time) and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good, with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters. Notation and presentation appear consistent and figures/tables/examples seem effective in conveying results (including sensitivity analysis). Minor presentation improvements could help clarify the more complex trade-offs around layer-wise sensitivity and iterative retraining, but overall the paper communicates its ideas clearly. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from methodology (L1-norm filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies and ResNet handling) to experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet and a concluding discussion, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters and comparisons to several baselines) are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables are implied to be effective given the reported FLOP, accuracy, and wall-clock results, and overall writing quality is described as good; minor presentation issues remain around communicating layer-sensitivity nuances and the practical tradeoff between one-shot and iterative retraining.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the summary, the paper is well organized with a clear flow from method (L1-norm filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies, ResNet handling) to experiments (FLOPs, accuracy, wall-clock time) and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good, with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters. Notation and presentation appear consistent and figures/tables/examples seem effective in conveying results (including sensitivity analysis). Minor presentation improvements could help clarify the more complex trade-offs around layer-wise sensitivity and iterative retraining, but overall the paper communicates its ideas clearly.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from methodology (L1-norm filter pruning, one-shot and stage-wise strategies and ResNet handling) to experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet and a concluding discussion, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters and comparisons to several baselines) are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables are implied to be effective given the reported FLOP, accuracy, and wall-clock results, and overall writing quality is described as good; minor presentation issues remain around communicating layer-sensitivity nuances and the practical tradeoff between one-shot and iterative retraining.

main_claims: Proposes pruning entire convolutional filters (and corresponding feature maps) based on the filters' L1-norm to reduce computation without introducing unstructured sparsity or requiring sparse libraries.

Employs a one-shot (prune once and retrain) and stage-wise pruning strategy (with independent and greedy selection variants) to efficiently prune multiple layers of deep networks, including practical handling for residual blocks in ResNets.

Demonstrates that this filter-pruning approach yields substantial FLOP reductions (≈30% reported for VGG on CIFAR-10 and for deep ResNets) with little loss in accuracy, and that sensitivity analysis can identify layers robust or sensitive to pruning.

Shows that pruning smallest-L1 filters outperforms pruning random or largest filters and compares favorably to activation-based feature-map pruning; provides practical results on wall-clock inference time matching FLOP reductions.
_summary.methodology: Compute the L1-norm (sum of absolute weights) for each convolutional filter, remove filters with smallest L1-norms (and their feature maps and corresponding kernels in the next layer), create a reduced model copying remaining weights, and retrain (one-shot or iteratively); for ResNets handle shortcut/projection constraints by pruning matched filters.
baselines: activation-based feature-map pruning (various statistics as in Polyak & Wolf 2015)
group-sparse / regularization approaches (discussed in related work)
limitations: Pruning filters from layers identified as sensitive can cause accuracy drops that may not be fully recovered by one-shot retraining.
Iterative prune-and-retrain can yield better recovery but requires many more epochs and is time-consuming for very deep networks.

Some layers/stages require different pruning rates (i.e., layer-wise sensitivity must be considered), and certain architectures (e.g., ResNet-34) are relatively harder to prune than deeper ResNets (reported empirically).
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - CNN model compression / filter pruning
